title: Thin Lens Equation Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Calculate focal length, object position, or image distance using the thin lens formula 1/f = 1/s + 1/s'. Free and instant.
intro: The Thin Lens Equation Calculator solves the fundamental relationship between object distance (s), image distance (s'), and focal length (f) for any thin lens. The governing formula is **1/f = 1/s + 1/s'**, where all distances are measured in centimeters (or any consistent unit) from the optical center of the lens. Use this tool whenever you need to find the missing third variable: given two of the three quantities (f, s, s'), the calculator instantly returns the third. It applies to converging (convex) lenses with positive focal lengths and diverging (concave) lenses with negative focal lengths, covering optics problems in physics courses, camera design, eyeglass prescription analysis, telescope and microscope layout, and laboratory bench setups.
keyTakeaway: The thin lens equation **1/f = 1/s + 1/s'** links object distance, image distance, and focal length. Example: s = 30 cm, s' = 20 cm → **f = 12 cm** (converging lens, real image).
useCases: Determining the focal length of an unknown converging lens in a physics lab by measuring object and image distances on an optical bench.
Calculating where to position a projector lens so that a slide at a fixed distance produces a sharp image on a screen 3 meters away.
Analyzing eyeglass prescriptions: converting a –2.50 diopter lens to its focal length (f = 1/–2.50 D = –40 cm) to predict image placement for a myopic eye.
Designing a simple telescope by computing the image distance produced by the objective lens, which then serves as the object distance input for the eyepiece lens.
Verifying that a camera macro lens set to a given focus distance will produce a 1:1 magnification image when the object is placed at exactly 2f from the lens.

example.title: Calculation Example
steps: Object distance s = 30 cm, Image distance s' = 20 cm

explanation:
## How It Is Calculated

The thin lens equation relates three quantities for any lens thin enough that its thickness is negligible compared to its focal length:

```
1/f = 1/s + 1/s'
```

| Variable | Symbol | Sign convention |
|----------|--------|-----------------|
| Focal length | f | + for converging (convex), – for diverging (concave) |
| Object distance | s | + when object is on the incoming-light side (real object) |
| Image distance | s' | + when image forms on the outgoing-light side (real image); – for virtual image |

**Solving for each variable:**

```
Find f:   f  = (s × s') / (s + s')
Find s:   s  = (f × s') / (s' – f)
Find s':  s' = (f × s)  / (s  – f)
```

**Magnification** (lateral, dimensionless):
```
m = –s' / s
```
A negative m means the image is inverted; |m| > 1 means it is enlarged.

**Lens power** (diopters, used in optometry):
```
P (D) = 1 / f (meters)
```

---

## Reference Table

| Scenario | s (cm) | s' (cm) | f (cm) | m | Image type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Object at 2f (symmetric) | 40 | 40 | 20 | –1.00 | Real, inverted, same size |
| Object beyond 2f | 30 | 20 | 12 | –0.67 | Real, inverted, reduced |
| Object between f and 2f | 15 | 60 | 12 | –4.00 | Real, inverted, enlarged |
| Object at f | 12 | ∞ | 12 | — | No image (parallel rays) |
| Object inside f (magnifier) | 8 | –24 | 12 | +3.00 | Virtual, upright, enlarged |
| Diverging lens, real object | 30 | –10 | –15 | +0.33 | Virtual, upright, reduced |
| Camera (standard 50 mm lens) | 5000 | 5.03 | 5 | –0.001 | Real, inverted, tiny |

---

## Typical Cases

### Case 1 — Physics Lab (find focal length)
A student places a candle 30 cm from a convex lens and observes a sharp image on a screen 20 cm on the other side.

```
1/f = 1/30 + 1/20
1/f = 2/60 + 3/60 = 5/60
f   = 60/5 = 12 cm
```

Magnification: m = –20/30 = –0.67 → image is real, inverted, and 33 % smaller.

### Case 2 — Projector Lens Design
A projector slide is 5.2 cm from a lens with f = 5 cm. Where must the screen be placed?

```
1/s' = 1/f – 1/s = 1/5 – 1/5.2
1/s' = 0.2000 – 0.1923 = 0.00769
s'   = 130 cm
```

Magnification: m = –130/5.2 = –25 → image is 25× enlarged (typical for a projector).

### Case 3 — Diverging (Concave) Lens
An object sits 40 cm from a concave lens with f = –20 cm. Find the image distance.

```
1/s' = 1/f – 1/s = 1/(–20) – 1/40
1/s' = –0.0500 – 0.0250 = –0.0750
s'   = –13.3 cm
```

The negative sign confirms a virtual image 13.3 cm on the same side as the object. Magnification: m = –(–13.3)/40 = +0.33 → upright, reduced.

---

## Common Errors

1. **Forgetting the sign convention for diverging lenses.** Plugging f = 20 cm instead of f = –20 cm for a concave lens produces a completely wrong image distance. Always assign negative f for diverging lenses.

2. **Mixing units (cm vs. m).** If s is in centimeters and f is entered in millimeters, the result is meaningless. Convert everything to the same unit before applying the formula. (Optometry uses meters to get diopters directly.)

3. **Confusing a virtual image distance as impossible.** A negative s' is not an error — it signals a virtual image forming on the same side as the incoming light, as seen through a magnifying glass or a diverging lens.

4. **Trying to find an image when the object is exactly at the focal point.** When s = f, the denominator (s – f) equals zero, making s' → ∞. Rays emerge parallel; no finite image exists.

5. **Neglecting the thin-lens assumption.** The formula breaks down for thick lenses (e.g., fish-eye camera lenses or the human crystalline lens). In those cases, the lensmaker's equation or ray-transfer matrices must be used instead.

6. **Ignoring lens aberrations in real-world design.** The thin lens equation gives the paraxial (small-angle) approximation. Real lenses suffer from spherical aberration, coma, and chromatic aberration, so calculated image positions may shift slightly from measured values.

---

## Related Calculators

Because no related slugs are available for this calculator at this time, explore other science tools on [Hacé Cuentas](/) for physics, optics, and engineering calculations.

faq.q: What is the thin lens equation and where does it come from?
faq.a: The thin lens equation 1/f = 1/s + 1/s' is derived from Snell's law applied twice at the two refracting surfaces of a lens, under the paraxial (small-angle) approximation where sin θ ≈ θ. It was formalized in its modern sign-convention form in the 19th century and is a cornerstone of geometric optics, described in detail by NIST and standard physics references. It assumes the lens thickness is negligible compared to the object and image distances.

faq.q: What is the difference between a converging and a diverging lens in this equation?
faq.a: A converging (convex) lens bends parallel rays to meet at a real focal point on the far side, giving a positive focal length (e.g., f = +10 cm). A diverging (concave) lens spreads rays as if they came from a virtual focal point behind the lens, giving a negative focal length (e.g., f = –15 cm). In the thin lens equation, the sign of f directly controls whether the resulting image is real or virtual and which side of the lens it forms on.

faq.q: How do I convert focal length to lens power in diopters (used for eyeglasses)?
faq.a: Lens power P (diopters, D) = 1 / f where f is in meters. A lens with f = +0.25 m (25 cm) has power P = +4.00 D (a reading glass). A myopia-correcting lens with f = –0.40 m (–40 cm) has P = –2.50 D. Optometrists use diopters because powers of lenses in contact add directly: P_total = P₁ + P₂, whereas focal lengths do not add simply.

faq.q: What does a negative image distance (s') mean physically?
faq.a: A negative s' means the image forms on the same side of the lens as the incoming light — it is a virtual image. Virtual images cannot be projected onto a screen but are visible to an observer looking through the lens. This occurs whenever the object is placed closer to a converging lens than its focal length (magnifying glass mode), or whenever a diverging lens is used with any real object distance.

faq.q: What is lateral magnification and how is it calculated from the thin lens equation?
faq.a: Lateral magnification m = –s'/s gives the ratio of image height to object height. A value of m = –3 means the image is three times taller than the object and inverted (real image). A value of m = +0.5 means the image is half the object's height and upright (virtual image). The negative sign arises from the standard Cartesian sign convention and indicates image inversion, not a physically smaller number.

faq.q: Can this calculator be used for mirrors as well as lenses?
faq.a: The mirror equation 1/f = 1/s + 1/s' is mathematically identical to the thin lens equation, so the same arithmetic applies. However, the sign conventions differ: for mirrors, a positive s' means a real image in front of the mirror, and the focal length of a concave (converging) mirror is f = R/2 where R is the radius of curvature. As long as you apply the correct mirror sign convention, this calculator gives the right numerical answer.

faq.q: Why does placing an object exactly at the focal length produce no image?
faq.a: When s = f, the thin lens equation gives 1/s' = 1/f – 1/f = 0, so s' → ∞. Physically, rays from an object at the focal point exit the lens as a parallel beam and never converge to form a finite image. This principle is exploited in collimating lenses (e.g., in laser systems and searchlights) to produce a beam of nearly parallel light from a point source.

faq.q: What assumptions make the thin lens equation break down in practice?
faq.a: The thin lens equation relies on three assumptions: (1) the lens thickness is much smaller than f, s, and s'; (2) only paraxial rays are considered (angles < ~5°); and (3) the lens is made of a single homogeneous material with no dispersion. Real lenses violate all three to some degree, producing spherical aberration, chromatic aberration, coma, and astigmatism. Precision optical designs use the lensmaker's equation, ray-tracing software, and multi-element lens systems to compensate.

sources.name: NIST: Optics and Photonics – Fundamental Constants and Equations

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data updates required
